questions.question: What makes the Spore NFT protocol different from most other NFT standards like ERC-721?
options: Spore NFTs can be transferred instantly without transaction fees because they use a Layer 2 rollup
Spore stores the actual NFT content (image bytes, text, SVG) directly in the cell's data field on CKB, rather than storing a URL pointing to an external server
Spore NFTs are automatically insured against theft by the CKB Foundation
Spore uses a shared IPFS cluster maintained by CKB validators so content is always available
questions.explanation: Spore's defining characteristic is on-chain content storage. While most NFT standards (ERC-721, ERC-1155, and many others) store only a URL in the token's metadata field — pointing to an external server, IPFS, or Arweave — Spore stores the actual bytes of the content (image data, text, SVG, JSON) directly in the CKB cell's data field. This means a Spore NFT's content is as permanent as the CKB blockchain itself. There is no external gateway that can go offline, no company that can change the content, and no URL that can break. The tradeoff is that on-chain storage costs CKBytes proportional to content size, which has led to a creative ecosystem focused on small, efficient formats like SVG and text.

questions.question: How is a Spore's unique ID generated, and why is this approach globally unique without a central registry?
options: An oracle service assigns sequential IDs from a global counter stored in a special registry cell
The Spore ID is randomly generated by the creator's wallet and checked against existing IDs via an RPC call
The Spore ID is computed from the first input cell's outpoint (tx_hash + index) in the creation transaction, and since each UTXO can only be consumed once, the ID is guaranteed unique
Each Spore cluster maintains its own sequential ID counter, so IDs are unique within a collection but may collide across collections
questions.explanation: Spore IDs use CKB's UTXO uniqueness guarantee. The ID is computed as blake2b(first_input_tx_hash ++ first_input_index) where the first_input is the outpoint of the first input cell consumed in the Spore creation transaction. Because CKB's UTXO model ensures each cell outpoint can only be consumed in exactly one transaction ever, this computation produces a value that can never be repeated. No two Spore creation transactions can have the same first input outpoint. This means Spore achieves global uniqueness without any central registry or coordinator — a property it inherits directly from the UTXO model.

questions.question: In a CKB NFT marketplace, what is a 'sale order cell' and what information does it contain?
options: A special type of Spore cell that has been marked for sale by adding a 'forsale' flag to its data field
A cell locked with the marketplace lock script whose args encode the spore_id being sold, the seller's address, and the asking price — announcing a listing without holding the NFT itself
A cell inside the Spore cluster contract that stores all active listings for that collection
A transaction in the mempool that has been broadcast to announce a sale but not yet included in a block
questions.explanation: A sale order cell is a separate cell from the Spore NFT itself. It is locked with the marketplace lock script, and its args encode three key pieces of information: the spore_id (which NFT is for sale), the seller_blake160 (who should receive the CKB payment), and the asking_price as a uint128 little-endian integer (minimum CKB to receive). The Spore cell itself is changed to also use the marketplace lock, but remains a separate cell. This separation keeps the Spore cell protocol-compliant (its Spore type script remains valid) while the order cell handles the marketplace mechanics. Any indexer scanning for the marketplace lock code_hash can find all active listings without executing code — just reading and decoding the args.

questions.question: When a buyer purchases an NFT on a CKB marketplace, what makes the transaction 'atomic'?
options: The marketplace smart contract uses a two-phase commit protocol to ensure both parties confirm before the transfer finalizes
A trusted escrow service holds both the NFT and the CKB payment until both parties sign a release
CKB transactions inherently consume all inputs and create all outputs simultaneously in one block — the NFT transfer and CKB payment happen in the same transaction with no intermediate state
The buyer and seller each sign half of the transaction, and the marketplace server combines them into one final transaction
questions.explanation: CKB transactions are atomic by construction. A buy transaction has the Spore cell and the sale order cell as inputs, and the buyer's new Spore cell plus the seller's payment cell as outputs. All inputs are consumed and all outputs are created simultaneously when the transaction is included in a block. There is no intermediate state: you cannot have a situation where the NFT is transferred but payment hasn't arrived, or payment is received but the NFT hasn't moved. The marketplace lock script validates all these conditions before approving the transaction. If any condition fails, the entire transaction is rejected. This atomicity is a property of the CKB transaction model itself, not something the marketplace developer needs to implement carefully.

questions.question: What is the total cost a buyer pays when purchasing a Spore NFT listed at 500 CKB, and where does each portion go?
options: Exactly 500 CKB: all of it goes to the seller as the asking price
500 CKB asking price to the seller, plus the Spore cell's capacity cost to the buyer (who receives the Spore cell with that capacity attached), plus a small transaction fee to miners
500 CKB to the seller plus a 2.5% platform fee to the marketplace protocol treasury
500 CKB split between the seller (95%) and liquidity providers (5%) who funded the marketplace
questions.explanation: The total buyer cost is asking_price + spore_cell_capacity + transaction_fee. The asking price (500 CKB) goes to the seller. The Spore cell's capacity (the CKBytes backing the NFT's on-chain storage) goes with the Spore cell to the buyer — the buyer now 'owns' both the NFT content and the storage capacity. This is an important property of CKB's model: when you receive a cell, you receive the capacity locked in it. The buyer effectively deposits the storage cost when taking ownership. There is no separate platform fee unless the marketplace lock script explicitly encodes one. Since the marketplace lock script is deployed code, its fee structure (or lack thereof) is transparent and immutable.

questions.question: How does `@ckb-ccc/connector-react` enable a React frontend to connect to different wallet types like JoyID, MetaMask, and hardware wallets?
options: The developer must write separate integration code for each wallet type and manually detect which wallet is installed
It works only with JoyID; other wallets require installing separate npm packages from each wallet provider

It provides a CccProvider component and a useCcc hook that expose a unified wallet interface; the provider handles wallet detection and presents the appropriate connection options based on the user's browser environment
It connects to a central CKB Foundation server that proxies all wallet interactions through a standardized API
questions.explanation: CCC connector-react abstracts wallet differences behind a unified interface. The CccProvider component wraps your application and handles wallet detection, connection management, and signing protocol differences across wallet types. The useCcc hook exposes a signer object that works identically regardless of whether the user is on JoyID (passkey-based), MetaMask (ECDSA via CKB plugin), Neuron (full node), or a hardware wallet (Ledger). Developers call signer.sendTransaction(tx) and the provider handles the underlying signing protocol. The provider also shows the appropriate wallet options to the user based on what is available in their browser, so a mobile user sees JoyID while a desktop user with MetaMask sees that option.

questions.question: Why is it important to verify transaction contents before calling signer.sendTransaction() in a marketplace frontend?
options: CCC does not validate transactions at all, so without frontend validation the transaction will always be rejected by nodes

A malicious listing or a compromised frontend could construct a transaction where the outputs drain the user's funds or transfer the wrong NFT; client-side validation catches these before the user signs
Transaction verification is only needed for token transfers, not for NFT purchases
The verification is purely for UX purposes — it displays a summary to the user but has no security implications
questions.explanation: Client-side transaction validation is a critical security layer. A malicious marketplace frontend, a compromised listing, or a bait-and-switch attack could construct a transaction with unexpected outputs: a seller substituting a different (cheaper) Spore for the one advertised, an output redirecting payment to the attacker's address, or extra outputs draining the buyer's CKBytes. By inspecting transaction outputs before signing — verifying the Spore ID matches what was displayed, confirming the seller address in the payment output matches the listing, checking no unexpected outputs exist — the frontend catches these attacks before the user's private key ever signs. CKB nodes will validate the lock script conditions, but they cannot detect that the 'correct' Spore ID was different from what the user intended to buy.

questions.question: What is the 'burn floor price' property of Spore NFTs, and what economic behavior does it create?
options: Spore NFTs have a mandatory minimum listing price set by the protocol to prevent undervaluation of CKB storage

Each Spore NFT has a built-in floor price equal to its capacity in CKB because burning the NFT permanently destroys it but releases the locked CKBytes back to the burner — making every Spore worth at least its storage cost
The Spore protocol takes a 1% burn fee from every transfer, creating deflationary pressure that increases the floor price over time
NFT platforms set the floor price by locking CKB in a reserve fund that can be claimed by buyers if the NFT value drops below a threshold
questions.explanation: Spore NFTs have a guaranteed minimum value equal to their on-chain storage cost because the Spore type script allows burning (permanent destruction) in exchange for releasing the locked CKBytes. A Spore cell with 500 CKB of capacity locked in it can always be burned to reclaim those 500 CKB, regardless of what the art market thinks of the content. This creates interesting economics: NFTs can never be truly worthless because they always retain their storage cost in CKB. It also gives creators a way to reclaim capital from unsold NFTs. The economic pressure encourages efficient content — small SVGs and text are cheaper to store than large images, creating an aesthetic community oriented toward minimalism and efficiency.
